figures.caption: Fig. 4. Architecture of the decentralized actor network. Each agent processes local observations through a shared torso to parameterize a Gaussian policy distribution.

Fig. 5. Architecture of the centralized multi-objective critic. The network processes the joint state of the swarm to produce independent value estimates for progression, energy efficiency, and smoothness objectives.

Fig. 9. Ablation study comparing per-episode training curves for the three reward objectives between the full MOMAPPO policy with PCGrad gradient surgery (blue) and an ablated variant without PCGrad (red), plotted as a function of global environment steps aggregated across all parallel training environments. All rewards are normalized by episode length. Shaded regions denote ±1 standard deviation across parallel environments. (Top) Progress reward, measuring upstream displacement against the fluid flow. (Middle) Energy efficiency reward, reflecting the alignment between applied thrust and resulting member displacement. (Bottom) Smoothness reward, quantifying temporal consistency of the action sequence via cosine similarity between consecutive actions. Without PCGrad, energy efficiency and smoothness collapse to near zero within 10,000 steps while progress reward exhibits persistent large-amplitude oscillations, demonstrating that naive gradient summation leads to destructive inter-objective interference and policy instability.

Fig. 8. Swarm behavior after policy learning. Snapshots of the x-velocity field and swarm member positions at twelve successive time points during a representative episode with the trained MOMAPPO policy, spanning a full triphasic flow cycle. Panels 1–6 (forward-flow phase): half the swarm anchors to the lower wall (y ≈ 0 mm) while the remaining members position themselves directly above, forming a two-layer obstruction that suppresses high-velocity jet formation and limits peak fluid velocity to approximately 400 mm/s; the swarm drifts downstream passively, remaining within the safe corridor (x < 80 mm). Panels 7–11 (reverse-flow phase): members disperse, re-anchor near the lower wall, and advance upstream in preparation for the subsequent forward pulse, implementing a cycle-synchronized ratchet mechanism. Panel 12 (final approach): individual members navigate independently toward the success boundary at x = 20 mm without apparent collective coordination. Arrow lengths and directions on each member indicate the instantaneous action vector.

Fig. 1. Illustrative use cases for magnetically actuated microrobotic swarms navigating fluid-filled conduits under an externally applied magnetic field (field lines shown). (Left) Biomedical application: a swarm of magnetic microrobots navigating against blood flow within a vessel, surrounded by red blood cells, representative of targeted drug delivery or minimally invasive vascular intervention scenarios. (Right) Industrial application: a swarm traversing a transparent fluid-filled channel, representative of inspection, sensing, or transport tasks in engineered microfluidic or pipeline systems. In both cases, the external magnetic field provides the actuation gradient that the learned policy exploits to coordinate upstream navigation against the ambient flow.

Fig. 3. Triphasic temporal velocity profile, representing the rapid systole, early diastolic flow reversal, and late diastolic forward flow characteristic of a healthy peripheral artery with a 400 mm/s peak systolic velocity.

Fig. 7. Swarm behavior before policy learning. Snapshots of the x-velocity field (color map, blue: negative; red: positive), swarm member positions (colored ellipses), and Center-Of-Mass of the swarm (black dot) at four successive time points during a representative episode with an untrained policy. The swarm initializes near x ≈ 50 mm and migrates collectively to the upper wall (y ≈ 2 mm), creating a channel constriction that generates high-velocity jets exceeding 700 mm/s. The resulting drag force advects the swarm past the episode-failure boundary at x = 80 mm, without any upstream recovery. Arrow lengths and directions on each member indicate the instantaneous action vector.
